topics.meaning: Integers are whole numbers together with their negatives, shown in order on a number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Integers are the collection of numbers that includes all the whole numbers (0, 1, 2, 3, ...) and their negatives (-1, -2, -3, ...). So the integers are ..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, .... We can show integers on a number line: zero is placed in the middle, positive integers are marked to the right of zero, and negative integers to the left. Each integer has its own fixed place on the line. The number line helps us see the order of integers and is useful for adding and subtracting them.
topics.easyExplanation: Integers are whole numbers plus their negatives: ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, .... They are shown on a number line with positives to the right and negatives to the left of 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What integers are
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Integers include whole numbers and their negatives.

stepByStepExplanation.title: On the number line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Integers are marked on a number line with 0 in the middle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Positives and negatives
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Positive integers go right of 0; negative integers go left.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fixed places
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each integer has its own fixed place on the number line.

examples: -3, 0 and 5 are all integers.
On the number line, -2 is just left of -1.

importantKeywords.definition: A whole number or its negative.
A line showing numbers in order.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Integers are marked on a number line.

topics.quickSummary: Integers are the whole numbers together with their negatives, shown in order on a number line with zero in the middle, positives to the right and negatives to the left.
revisionNotes: Shown on a number line, 0 in the middle.

topics.meaning: On the number line, an integer to the right is greater, which helps us compare and order integers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To compare integers, we use their positions on the number line. The rule is simple: an integer that lies further to the right is greater, and one that lies further to the left is smaller. So every positive integer is greater than zero, and zero is greater than every negative integer; this means positive integers are always greater than negative integers. Among negative integers, the one closer to zero is greater, so -1 is greater than -5. Using these rules, we can compare any two integers and arrange a set of integers in ascending (smallest to largest) or descending order.
topics.easyExplanation: On the number line, an integer to the right is greater. So 3 > -5, and among negatives -1 > -5. This lets us order integers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the number line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Compare integers using their positions on the number line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Right is greater
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An integer further right is greater; further left is smaller.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Positives vs negatives
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every positive integer is greater than every negative integer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Among negatives
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The negative integer closer to zero is greater, so -1 > -5.

examples: 3 is greater than -5 because 3 is to the right.
-1 is greater than -5 because it is closer to zero.
topics.quickSummary: Integers are compared using the number line, where an integer to the right is greater; positives exceed negatives, and among negatives the one nearer zero is greater, letting us order integers.
revisionNotes: Used to order in ascending/descending order.

topics.meaning: Integers are added using rules of signs, which can be pictured on a number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To add integers we use rules about their signs. To add two integers with the same sign, we add their values and keep the common sign; for example, (-3) + (-4) = -7 and 3 + 4 = 7. To add two integers with different signs, we subtract the smaller value from the larger and take the sign of the larger; for example, (-7) + 5 = -2 and 7 + (-5) = 2. On a number line, adding a positive integer means moving to the right, and adding a negative integer means moving to the left. These rules let us add any integers confidently.
topics.easyExplanation: To add integers: same signs add and keep the sign (-3 + -4 = -7); different signs subtract and take the larger's sign (-7 + 5 = -2).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the values and keep the common sign, like -3 + -4 = -7.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Different signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract the smaller value from the larger and take the larger's sign.

stepByStepExplanation.title: On the number line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Adding a positive moves right; adding a negative moves left.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Any integers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These rules let us add any two integers.

topics.quickSummary: Integers are added by adding same-sign values and keeping the sign, or subtracting different-sign values and taking the larger's sign, which can be pictured by moving on a number line.
revisionNotes: Same signs: add, keep the sign.
Different signs: subtract, take larger's sign.
On number line: + moves right, - moves left.

topics.meaning: To subtract an integer, we add its additive inverse (its opposite).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Subtracting an integer is done by changing it into an addition. The rule is: to subtract an integer, add its additive inverse (its opposite). The additive inverse of a number is the number that adds to it to give zero; for example, the additive inverse of 3 is -3, and the additive inverse of -5 is 5. So, to subtract, we change the sign of the integer being subtracted and then add. For example, 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8, and (-4) - 6 = (-4) + (-6) = -10. Once we change subtraction into addition, we use the rules of addition. This makes subtraction of integers simple and reliable.
topics.easyExplanation: To subtract an integer, add its opposite. So 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8, and -4 - 6 = -4 + (-6) = -10.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change to addition
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To subtract an integer, add its additive inverse (opposite).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Additive inverse
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The opposite of a number adds to it to give zero, like 3 and -3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change the sign
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Change the sign of the integer being subtracted, then add.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use addition rules
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: After changing to addition, use the rules for adding integers.

importantKeywords.definition: A number with the opposite sign.

topics.quickSummary: To subtract an integer, we add its additive inverse (opposite): we change the sign of the integer being subtracted and then add, so 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8.

topics.topic: Properties of Addition and Subtraction of Integers
topics.meaning: Integer addition is closed, commutative and associative, and zero is the additive identity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Integers follow several useful properties under addition. Closure: the sum of two integers is always an integer. Commutative property: the order does not matter, so a + b = b + a, for example 3 + (-5) = (-5) + 3. Associative property: when adding three integers, the grouping does not matter, so (a + b) + c = a + (b + c). Additive identity: adding zero does not change an integer, so a + 0 = a; zero is called the additive identity. Subtraction of integers is closed (the difference is always an integer) but is not commutative or associative. These properties help us add integers easily and in any convenient order.
topics.easyExplanation: Integer addition is closed, commutative (a+b=b+a) and associative, and adding 0 changes nothing (identity). Subtraction is closed but not commutative.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Closure
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The sum of two integers is always an integer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Commutative
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Order does not matter: a + b = b + a.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Associative
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Grouping does not matter: (a+b)+c = a+(b+c).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Additive identity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Adding 0 changes nothing: a + 0 = a.

examples: 3 + (-5) = (-5) + 3 shows the commutative property.
7 + 0 = 7 shows zero is the additive identity.

importantKeywords.definition: Order does not change the result.
Grouping does not change the result.
The number 0 that does not change a sum.

topics.quickSummary: Integer addition is closed, commutative and associative, and zero is the additive identity; subtraction is closed but not commutative or associative.
revisionNotes: Zero is the additive identity (a + 0 = a).
Subtraction is closed but not commutative.

topics.meaning: When multiplying integers, like signs give a positive product and unlike signs give a negative product.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we multiply integers, the sign of the answer depends on the signs of the numbers. The product of two positive integers is positive, and the product of two negative integers is also positive (like signs give a positive). The product of a positive and a negative integer is negative (unlike signs give a negative). For example, (-4) x (-3) = 12, but (-4) x 3 = -12 and 4 x (-3) = -12. To find the product, we multiply the values and then apply the sign rule. A useful note: the product of an even number of negative integers is positive, and of an odd number of negative integers is negative.
topics.easyExplanation: Multiplying integers: like signs (++ or --) give a positive answer; unlike signs give a negative answer. So (-4) x (-3) = 12 but (-4) x 3 = -12.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Like signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two positives or two negatives give a positive product.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unlike signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A positive and a negative give a negative product.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the values, then apply the sign rule.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Many negatives
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Even number of negatives = positive; odd number = negative.

topics.quickSummary: When multiplying integers, like signs give a positive product and unlike signs give a negative product; an even number of negative factors gives a positive product and an odd number a negative.

topics.meaning: Integer multiplication is closed, commutative and associative, has 1 as identity, and is distributive over addition.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Integers follow useful properties under multiplication. Closure: the product of two integers is always an integer. Commutative: order does not matter, a x b = b x a. Associative: grouping does not matter, (a x b) x c = a x (b x c). Multiplicative identity: multiplying by 1 does not change an integer, a x 1 = a. Multiplying any integer by 0 gives 0. Distributive property: multiplication distributes over addition, a x (b + c) = a x b + a x c, which is very useful for calculations. These properties let us multiply integers in any convenient order and simplify expressions.
topics.easyExplanation: Integer multiplication is closed, commutative (a x b = b x a) and associative; 1 is the identity, x0 = 0, and it is distributive: a x (b+c) = a x b + a x c.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Closure and order
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Product is always an integer; a x b = b x a (commutative).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Associative
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Grouping does not matter: (a x b) x c = a x (b x c).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identity and zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: a x 1 = a (identity); a x 0 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Distributive
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: a x (b + c) = a x b + a x c.

examples: (-3) x 5 = 5 x (-3) shows commutativity.
2 x (3 + 4) = 2x3 + 2x4 shows the distributive property.

importantKeywords.definition: Order does not change the product.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: a x b = b x a.

importantKeywords.definition: The number 1 that does not change a product.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: a x (b+c) = a x b + a x c.

topics.quickSummary: Integer multiplication is closed, commutative and associative, has 1 as the multiplicative identity, gives 0 when multiplied by 0, and is distributive over addition.
revisionNotes: Distributive: a x (b+c) = a x b + a x c.
Helps multiply in any order and simplify.

topics.meaning: When dividing integers, like signs give a positive quotient and unlike signs give a negative quotient.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Division of integers follows the same sign rules as multiplication. When we divide two integers with like signs (both positive or both negative), the quotient is positive; when we divide two integers with unlike signs, the quotient is negative. For example, (-12) ÷ (-3) = 4, but (-12) ÷ 3 = -4 and 12 ÷ (-3) = -4. To divide, we divide the values and then apply the sign rule. We can never divide by zero. Also, dividing an integer by 1 gives the same integer, and dividing a non-zero integer by itself gives 1. Understanding division lets us solve a wider range of integer problems.
topics.easyExplanation: Dividing integers: like signs give a positive answer; unlike signs give a negative answer. So (-12) ÷ (-3) = 4. We can never divide by zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Like signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dividing two integers with the same sign gives a positive quotient.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unlike signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dividing two integers with different signs gives a negative quotient.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the quotient
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide the values, then apply the sign rule.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Special cases
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Never divide by zero; a ÷ 1 = a and a ÷ a = 1 (a not 0).

topics.quickSummary: When dividing integers, like signs give a positive quotient and unlike signs give a negative quotient; we can never divide by zero, and dividing by 1 or by itself gives simple results.

topics.meaning: Fractions can be proper, improper or mixed, and like or unlike, depending on their numerators and denominators.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A fraction shows a part of a whole and is written as a numerator over a denominator. Fractions are of different types. A proper fraction has a numerator smaller than its denominator and is less than 1, like 3/4. An improper fraction has a numerator equal to or greater than its denominator and is 1 or more, like 7/4. A mixed fraction has a whole number part and a proper fraction, like 1 3/4, and every improper fraction can be written as a mixed fraction. Fractions with the same denominator are called like fractions, and those with different denominators are unlike fractions. Knowing these types helps us work with fractions correctly.
topics.easyExplanation: Fractions can be proper (3/4), improper (7/4) or mixed (1 3/4). Like fractions have the same denominator; unlike fractions have different denominators.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Proper fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Numerator smaller than denominator, less than 1, like 3/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Improper fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Numerator at least the denominator, 1 or more, like 7/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mixed fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A whole number with a proper fraction, like 1 3/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Like and unlike
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Like fractions share a denominator; unlike fractions differ.

examples: 3/4 is a proper fraction; 7/4 is improper and equals 1 3/4.
2/5 and 3/5 are like fractions.

importantKeywords.definition: A fraction equal to or greater than 1.
A whole number with a proper fraction.

topics.quickSummary: Fractions can be proper (less than 1), improper (1 or more) or mixed (whole plus fraction), and like (same denominator) or unlike (different denominator).
revisionNotes: Like share a denominator; unlike differ.

topics.meaning: Equivalent fractions show the same value, and fractions are compared using a common denominator.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Equivalent fractions are fractions that look different but show the same value, like 1/2, 2/4 and 3/6. We make an equivalent fraction by multiplying or dividing both the numerator and denominator by the same number. To compare fractions, it is easiest when they have the same denominator: then the one with the larger numerator is greater. If the denominators are different, we change the fractions into equivalent fractions with a common denominator and then compare the numerators. For fractions with the same numerator, the one with the smaller denominator is greater. Comparing lets us order fractions.
topics.easyExplanation: Equivalent fractions show the same value (1/2 = 2/4). To compare, use a common denominator: the bigger numerator wins.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Equivalent fractions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Different fractions with the same value, like 1/2 and 2/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Making them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply or divide both parts by the same number.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare with common denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Same denominator: the larger numerator is greater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same numerator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: With equal numerators, the smaller denominator is greater.

examples: 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 are equivalent fractions.
3/5 is greater than 2/5.

importantKeywords.definition: Different fractions with the same value.
The same denominator for two fractions.

topics.quickSummary: Equivalent fractions show the same value (made by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number), and fractions are compared using a common denominator, where the larger numerator is greater.
revisionNotes: Multiply/divide both parts by the same number.

topics.meaning: Fractions are added or subtracted by giving them a common denominator and then adding or subtracting the numerators.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To add or subtract fractions, they must have the same denominator. If the denominators are already the same (like fractions), we simply add or subtract the numerators and keep the denominator unchanged; for example, 1/5 + 2/5 = 3/5. If the denominators are different (unlike fractions), we first change them into equivalent fractions with a common denominator, and then add or subtract the numerators. For mixed fractions, we can add the whole parts and the fraction parts separately, or first convert to improper fractions. The final answer should be written in its simplest form.
topics.easyExplanation: To add or subtract fractions, make the denominators the same, then add or subtract the numerators. So 1/2 + 1/3 = 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Need same denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fractions can be added/subtracted only with the same denominator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Like fractions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add or subtract the numerators, keep the denominator: 1/5 + 2/5 = 3/5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unlike fractions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: First make a common denominator, then add or subtract.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the answer in its simplest form.

topics.quickSummary: Fractions are added or subtracted by giving them a common denominator and then adding or subtracting the numerators, with the answer written in its simplest form.
revisionNotes: Unlike: make a common denominator first.

topics.meaning: Fractions are multiplied by multiplying the numerators together and the denominators together.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply two fractions, we multiply the numerators (top numbers) together to get the new numerator, and multiply the denominators (bottom numbers) together to get the new denominator, then simplify; for example, (2/3) x (4/5) = 8/15. To multiply a fraction by a whole number, we multiply the whole number with the numerator. The word 'of' in problems means multiplication, so '1/2 of 10' means (1/2) x 10 = 5. An interesting feature is that when we multiply a proper fraction by another proper fraction, the product is smaller than both fractions.
topics.easyExplanation: To multiply fractions, multiply the tops together and the bottoms together, then simplify. So (2/3) x (4/5) = 8/15. 'Of' means multiply.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply numerators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the top numbers to get the new numerator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply denominators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the bottom numbers to get the new denominator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reduce the answer to its simplest form.

importantKeywords.definition: The top number of a fraction.
The bottom number of a fraction.

topics.quickSummary: Fractions are multiplied by multiplying the numerators together and the denominators together, then simplifying; the word 'of' means multiplication.
revisionNotes: Proper x proper is smaller than both.

topics.meaning: To divide by a fraction, we multiply by its reciprocal (the fraction turned upside down).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Dividing by a fraction is done by multiplying by its reciprocal. The reciprocal of a fraction is found by interchanging its numerator and denominator; for example, the reciprocal of 3/4 is 4/3. To divide one fraction by another, we keep the first fraction, change the division sign to multiplication, and use the reciprocal of the second fraction; for example, (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 10/12 = 5/6. The same method works to divide a whole number by a fraction or a fraction by a whole number. Knowing the reciprocal makes division of fractions simple.
topics.easyExplanation: To divide by a fraction, flip it (its reciprocal) and multiply. So (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 5/6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the reciprocal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The reciprocal interchanges the numerator and denominator, like 3/4 to 4/3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change to multiplication
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep the first fraction and change ÷ to x.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply by the reciprocal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply by the reciprocal of the second fraction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Simplify the answer.

examples: The reciprocal of 3/4 is 4/3.
topics.quickSummary: To divide by a fraction we multiply by its reciprocal (numerator and denominator interchanged), so (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 5/6.
revisionNotes: Divide by a fraction = multiply by its reciprocal.

topics.meaning: Decimals are numbers with a decimal point that show tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A decimal number is a number that has a whole-number part and a fractional part, separated by a decimal point, like 2.45. The digits after the decimal point show tenths, hundredths, thousandths and so on; for example, in 2.45 the 4 is in the tenths place and the 5 is in the hundredths place. Decimals are closely related to fractions: 0.5 is the same as 1/2, and 0.25 is the same as 1/4. We can write a fraction as a decimal and a decimal as a fraction. Decimals are used everywhere, for example in money and measurements, because they make it easy to write and compare values that are not whole numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Decimals have a whole part and a fractional part separated by a point, like 2.45. The places after the point are tenths, hundredths and thousandths. 0.5 = 1/2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What a decimal is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A decimal has a whole-number part and a fractional part separated by a point.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Place value
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Digits after the point are tenths, hundredths and thousandths.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Linked to fractions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 0.5 = 1/2 and 0.25 = 1/4; decimals and fractions are related.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Used everywhere
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decimals are used in money and measurements.

examples: In 2.45, the 4 is in the tenths place and the 5 in the hundredths place.
0.5 is the same as 1/2.

importantKeywords.definition: A number with a decimal point.
The first place after the decimal point.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: In 2.4, 4 is in the tenths place.

topics.quickSummary: Decimals are numbers with a whole part and a fractional part separated by a decimal point, where places after the point show tenths, hundredths and thousandths, and they are linked to fractions.

topics.meaning: Decimals are multiplied like whole numbers, then the decimal point is placed by counting decimal places.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply decimal numbers, we first multiply them as if they were whole numbers, ignoring the decimal points. Then we count the total number of digits after the decimal points in both numbers, and place the decimal point in the answer so that it has that many decimal places. For example, 2.5 x 1.2: multiplying 25 x 12 = 300, and there are two decimal places in total, so the answer is 3.00 = 3. To multiply a decimal by 10, 100 or 1000, we simply move the decimal point one, two or three places to the right; for example, 3.4 x 10 = 34.
topics.easyExplanation: Multiply decimals like whole numbers, then count the total decimal places and place the point. Multiplying by 10, 100, 1000 moves the point right.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply as whole numbers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ignore the decimal points and multiply as whole numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count decimal places
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Count the total digits after the decimal points in both numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Place the point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put the decimal point so the answer has that many decimal places.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By 10, 100, 1000
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move the decimal point one, two or three places to the right.

examples: 2.5 x 1.2 = 3 (25 x 12 = 300, two decimal places).

importantKeywords.definition: A number with a decimal point.

topics.quickSummary: Decimals are multiplied like whole numbers, then the decimal point is placed by counting the total decimal places; multiplying by 10, 100 or 1000 moves the point right.
revisionNotes: Multiply decimals as whole numbers first.
Count total decimal places to place the point.

topics.meaning: Decimals are divided by adjusting the decimal point, especially by making the divisor a whole number.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To divide decimals, we handle the decimal point carefully. To divide a decimal by 10, 100 or 1000, we move the decimal point one, two or three places to the left; for example, 4.5 ÷ 10 = 0.45. To divide a decimal by a whole number, we divide as usual and place the decimal point in the quotient directly above its place in the dividend. To divide a decimal by another decimal, we first make the divisor a whole number by multiplying both numbers by 10, 100 or 1000, and then divide. For example, 2.5 ÷ 0.5 becomes 25 ÷ 5 = 5. These methods let us divide decimals accurately.
topics.easyExplanation: To divide decimals: by 10, 100, 1000 move the point left; by a whole number divide normally; by a decimal, first make the divisor a whole number.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By 10, 100, 1000
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move the decimal point left, like 4.5 ÷ 10 = 0.45.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By a whole number
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide as usual and place the point above its place in the dividend.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By a decimal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Make the divisor a whole number by multiplying both by 10, 100 or 1000.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Then divide
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide the adjusted numbers to get the answer.

topics.quickSummary: Decimals are divided by adjusting the decimal point: by 10, 100, 1000 move it left; by a whole number divide normally; by a decimal, first make the divisor a whole number.
revisionNotes: By a whole number: divide, place point above.
By a decimal: make divisor a whole number first.

topics.meaning: Data is information collected for a purpose, and it is organised in tables to study it easily.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Data is a collection of numbers or information gathered for a particular purpose, like the marks of students or runs scored in matches. Raw data, collected as it comes, is hard to read, so we organise it. We arrange data in order and use a frequency distribution table with tally marks to show how many times each value occurs (its frequency). The difference between the highest and lowest values is the range. Organising data well is the first step to understanding it and drawing useful conclusions.
topics.easyExplanation: Data is collected information, like students' marks. We organise it in tables with tally marks and frequencies so it is easy to read and study.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What data is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Data is numbers or information collected for a purpose.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Organise it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Raw data is arranged in order and put into a table.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Frequency
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tally marks show how many times each value occurs.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Range
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The difference between the highest and lowest values.

examples: The marks of 20 students form a set of data.
A frequency table shows how many students scored each mark.

importantKeywords.definition: How many times a value occurs.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A mark by 5 students has frequency 5.

topics.quickSummary: Data is information collected for a purpose, organised in frequency tables with tally marks to show how often each value occurs, which makes it easy to study.
revisionNotes: Organise in tables with tally marks.
Frequency = how often a value occurs.

topics.meaning: The arithmetic mean (average) is found by adding all the values and dividing by the number of values.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The arithmetic mean, often just called the average or mean, is one way to represent a whole set of data by a single value. It is found by adding all the values together and then dividing by the number of values. For example, the mean of 2, 4 and 6 is (2 + 4 + 6) divided by 3, which is 12/3 = 4. The mean is a kind of 'balancing point' of the data and always lies between the smallest and largest values. The mean is very useful for comparing groups, such as the average marks of two classes. It is the most commonly used representative value.
topics.easyExplanation: The mean (average) = sum of all values ÷ number of values. The mean of 2, 4, 6 is 12/3 = 4. It lies between the smallest and largest values.

topics.meaning: The mode is the value that occurs most often in a set of data.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The mode is another representative value of data. The mode is simply the value that appears the greatest number of times, that is, the one with the highest frequency. For example, in the data 2, 3, 3, 5, 3, 7, the value 3 appears most often, so the mode is 3. A set of data can have one mode, more than one mode, or sometimes no mode at all. The mode is especially useful when we want to know the most common or most popular item, such as the most common shoe size sold in a shop. The mode is easy to find from a frequency table by looking for the highest frequency.
topics.easyExplanation: The mode is the value that occurs most often. In 2, 3, 3, 5, 3, the mode is 3. It is useful for finding the most common item.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The mode tells us the most common or popular item.

topics.meaning: The median is the middle value of data when it is arranged in order.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The median is a representative value that gives the middle of the data. To find the median, we first arrange the data in order (ascending or descending). The median is then the value exactly in the middle. If there is an odd number of values, the median is the single middle value; for example, the median of 3, 5, 7 is 5. If there is an even number of values, the median is the average of the two middle values; for example, the median of 3, 5, 7, 9 is (5+7)/2 = 6. The median is useful because it is not affected by very large or very small values in the data.

topics.meaning: A bar graph shows data using bars, making it easy to compare quantities at a glance.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A bar graph represents data using rectangular bars of equal width, where the length or height of each bar shows the value it represents. The bars are drawn with equal gaps on a graph with two axes and a suitable scale. Longer bars mean larger values, so we can compare different quantities just by looking at the bars. To draw a bar graph, we mark the items on one axis and the values on the other using a scale, then draw a bar for each item to the correct height. Bar graphs present data clearly and are widely used in newspapers, reports and textbooks.

topics.meaning: A double bar graph uses two bars side by side to compare two sets of data at once.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A double bar graph is a special kind of bar graph used to compare two sets of data at the same time. Instead of one bar for each item, it has two bars drawn side by side for each item, usually in different colours, with a key (legend) to show what each colour stands for. For example, a double bar graph can compare the marks of two students across several subjects, or the rainfall of two years across several months. By placing the two bars next to each other, we can easily compare the two sets of data item by item. Double bar graphs are very useful for comparison.

topics.meaning: Probability tells us how likely an event is to happen, on a scale from impossible to certain.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In daily life some events are certain, some are impossible, and many are uncertain. Probability is a way of measuring how likely an uncertain event is to happen. For an experiment with equally likely outcomes, the probability of an event is the number of favourable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. For example, when tossing a coin there are two outcomes (head or tail), so the probability of getting a head is 1/2; when rolling a die, the probability of getting a 3 is 1/6. Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Understanding chance helps us reason about games, weather and many real-life situations.
topics.easyExplanation: Probability measures how likely an event is, from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Tossing a coin, the chance of a head is 1/2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Calculate it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Probability = favourable outcomes / total outcomes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The scale
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

The probability of rolling a 3 on a die is 1/6.

topics.meaning: We use organised data and graphs to read information, compare values and make decisions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Once data has been organised and shown in a table or a graph, we can interpret it, that is, read and understand the information it gives. From a bar graph we can quickly see which item has the highest or lowest value and compare items. From the mean, median and mode we can describe the typical value of the data. From a double bar graph we can compare two groups. Interpreting data helps people make decisions, for example a shopkeeper deciding which items to stock, or a weather office reporting rainfall. The whole purpose of data handling is to collect, organise, represent and interpret data so that it gives us useful information.

topics.meaning: A variable is a letter that stands for an unknown number, and combining variables and numbers gives an expression.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In algebra, we use letters like x, y or a to stand for numbers we do not yet know; such a letter is called a variable, because its value can vary or change. A number on its own, whose value is fixed, is called a constant. When we combine variables and constants using operations like addition, subtraction and multiplication, we get an algebraic expression, such as 3x + 5 or 2y - 7. Expressions are the building blocks of equations. Understanding variables and expressions is the first step to writing and solving equations, which let us find unknown quantities.
topics.easyExplanation: A variable (like x) stands for an unknown number; a constant has a fixed value. Combining them gives an expression like 3x + 5.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A letter like x that stands for an unknown number.

topics.meaning: An equation is a statement that two expressions are equal, joined by an equals sign.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An equation is a mathematical statement that says two expressions have the same value, shown by an equals sign (=). At least one side usually contains a variable, an unknown quantity. For example, x + 3 = 8 is an equation: the left side, x + 3, equals the right side, 8. An equation is like a balance: the two sides must stay equal. The value of the variable that makes the two sides equal is called the solution of the equation. An equation is different from an expression, because an expression has no equals sign. Equations are powerful tools for finding unknown quantities.
topics.easyExplanation: An equation says two expressions are equal, like x + 3 = 8, joined by '='. It is like a balance; the value that makes both sides equal is the solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What an equation is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An equation states that two expressions are equal, with an equals sign.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The solution
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The value of the variable that makes both sides equal is the solution.

In x + 3 = 8, the solution is x = 5.

topics.meaning: We can turn a word problem or a statement into an equation by using a variable for the unknown.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many real-life situations can be written as equations. To set up an equation from a statement, we first decide what is unknown and represent it with a variable, such as x. Then we read the statement carefully and write the given condition as a mathematical equation. For example, 'a number increased by 5 is 12' becomes x + 5 = 12; 'three times a number is 18' becomes 3x = 18; and 'four less than twice a number is 10' becomes 2x - 4 = 10. Setting up the correct equation is an important skill, because once the equation is written correctly, it can be solved to find the unknown.
topics.easyExplanation: To set up an equation, use a variable for the unknown and write the condition as a maths statement. 'A number plus 5 is 12' becomes x + 5 = 12.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the unknown
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decide what is unknown and represent it with a variable like x.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the condition as a mathematical equation.

topics.meaning: An equation is solved by doing the same operation on both sides to keep it balanced.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To solve an equation means to find the value of the variable that makes it true. One method is to keep the equation balanced, like a pair of scales: whatever operation we do to one side, we must do the same to the other side, so that both sides stay equal. We can add, subtract, multiply or divide both sides by the same number to gradually get the variable alone on one side. For example, to solve x + 3 = 8, we subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5; to solve 2x = 10, we divide both sides by 2 to get x = 5. This balancing method is reliable and helps us understand why solving works.
topics.easyExplanation: To solve an equation, do the same operation to both sides to keep it balanced. For x + 3 = 8, subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Keep it balanced
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Whatever you do to one side, do to the other side.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Isolate the variable

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: x + 3 = 8: subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 2x = 10: divide both sides by 2 to get x = 5.

topics.meaning: Transposing means moving a term to the other side of the equation and changing its sign or operation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Another quick method to solve an equation is transposing. Transposing means moving a term from one side of the equation to the other side. When a term is transposed, its sign changes: a term that is added on one side becomes subtracted on the other, and a term that is multiplied becomes divided. For example, in x + 3 = 8, we transpose +3 to the right side to get x = 8 - 3 = 5; in 2x = 10, we transpose the multiplication by 2 to get x = 10/2 = 5. Transposing is really a shortcut for the balancing method and makes solving equations faster once we are comfortable with it.
topics.easyExplanation: Transposing means moving a term to the other side and changing its sign or operation. x + 3 = 8 becomes x = 8 - 3 = 5.

topics.meaning: We check a solution by substituting it back into the equation to see if both sides are equal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After solving an equation, it is good practice to check whether our answer is correct. We do this by substituting (putting in) the value we found for the variable back into the original equation, and then seeing whether the left side equals the right side. If both sides come out equal, our solution is correct; if they do not, we have made a mistake and need to solve again. For example, for x + 3 = 8 we found x = 5; checking, 5 + 3 = 8, which is true, so x = 5 is correct. Checking the solution helps us catch errors and be confident in our answer.
topics.easyExplanation: We check a solution by putting the value back into the equation. For x + 3 = 8 with x = 5: 5 + 3 = 8 is true, so x = 5 is correct.

topics.meaning: Simple equations are used to solve real-life word problems involving an unknown quantity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Simple equations are very useful for solving everyday problems where some quantity is unknown. To solve a word problem, we read it carefully, choose a variable for the unknown, write an equation from the given conditions, solve the equation, and then state the answer in words. For example, 'Three times a number plus 4 is 19' becomes 3x + 4 = 19, which gives x = 5. Equations can be used to find unknown ages, prices, lengths and many other quantities. We should always check that our answer fits the original problem. This shows how algebra turns real situations into equations and finds the unknown.

topics.meaning: We can also work backwards, building an equation that has a given solution.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Usually we are given an equation and asked to find its solution. But we can also work the other way round: starting from a known value of the variable, we can build different equations that have that value as their solution. For example, starting from x = 5, we can build the equation x + 2 = 7 (since 5 + 2 = 7), or 3x = 15, or 2x - 1 = 9, all of which have x = 5 as their solution. We do this by doing the same operations to both sides, starting from x = 5. This shows that many different equations can have the same solution, and it deepens our understanding of how equations and their solutions are connected.

topics.meaning: Points, line segments, lines, rays and angles are the basic building blocks of geometry.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Geometry is built from a few basic ideas. A point marks an exact position and has no size. A line segment is the straight path between two endpoints and has a fixed length. A line is a straight path that extends endlessly in both directions. A ray starts at one point and extends endlessly in one direction. An angle is formed when two rays start from the same point, called the vertex; the two rays are the arms of the angle. Angles are measured in degrees using a protractor. These basic ideas are the foundation for studying lines and angles.

topics.meaning: Two angles are complementary if the sum of their measures is 90 degrees.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When the measures of two angles add up to 90 degrees, the two angles are called complementary angles, and each angle is called the complement of the other. For example, 30 degrees and 60 degrees are complementary because 30 + 60 = 90; so the complement of 30 degrees is 60 degrees. If we know one of two complementary angles, we can find the other by subtracting it from 90 degrees. The two complementary angles may be placed together to form a right angle, or they may be separate. Recognising complementary angles helps us find unknown angles in many geometry problems.
topics.easyExplanation: Two angles are complementary if they add up to 90 degrees. So 30 and 60 are complementary; the complement of 40 is 50.

topics.meaning: Two angles are supplementary if the sum of their measures is 180 degrees.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When the measures of two angles add up to 180 degrees, the two angles are called supplementary angles, and each is called the supplement of the other. For example, 110 degrees and 70 degrees are supplementary because 110 + 70 = 180; so the supplement of 110 degrees is 70 degrees. If we know one of two supplementary angles, we can find the other by subtracting it from 180 degrees. Two supplementary angles placed together form a straight angle (a straight line). Recognising supplementary angles is very useful for finding unknown angles, especially along straight lines.
topics.easyExplanation: Two angles are supplementary if they add up to 180 degrees. So 110 and 70 are supplementary; the supplement of 120 is 60.

topics.meaning: Adjacent angles share a vertex and an arm, and a linear pair is adjacent angles that add up to 180 degrees.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two angles are called adjacent angles if they have a common vertex and a common arm, and their other arms are on opposite sides of the common arm, so that they do not overlap. A special case of adjacent angles is the linear pair: a linear pair is a pair of adjacent angles whose non-common arms form a straight line. The two angles of a linear pair always add up to 180 degrees, so they are supplementary. For example, when a ray stands on a line, the two angles formed are a linear pair. Adjacent angles and linear pairs help us find unknown angles in figures.

topics.meaning: When two lines intersect, the angles opposite each other are equal and are called vertically opposite angles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When two straight lines cross (intersect) each other at a point, they form four angles. The angles that are opposite to each other at the crossing point are called vertically opposite angles. An important property is that vertically opposite angles are always equal. So when two lines intersect, there are two pairs of vertically opposite angles, and the angles in each pair are equal. For example, if one of the four angles is 70 degrees, the angle opposite to it is also 70 degrees, and the other two angles are each 110 degrees. This property is very useful for finding unknown angles when lines cross.
topics.easyExplanation: When two lines cross, the angles opposite each other are vertically opposite angles, and they are always equal.

topics.topic: Pairs of Lines: Intersecting and Parallel
topics.meaning: Lines can be intersecting (crossing at a point) or parallel (never meeting).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we look at two lines, they can relate to each other in different ways. Two lines that cross each other at a point are called intersecting lines, and the point where they meet is the point of intersection. Two lines in the same plane that never meet, no matter how far they are extended in both directions, are called parallel lines, and they always stay the same distance apart. The rails of a railway track and the opposite edges of a ruler are examples of parallel lines. Understanding intersecting and parallel lines is important for studying the angles formed when a third line crosses them.
topics.easyExplanation: Two lines that cross at a point are intersecting; two lines that never meet are parallel. Railway tracks are parallel; crossing roads are intersecting.

topics.topic: Transversal and the Angles it Makes
topics.meaning: A transversal is a line that cuts two or more lines, forming several pairs of angles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A transversal is a line that crosses two or more lines at different points. When a transversal cuts two lines, it forms eight angles, which can be grouped into special pairs. Corresponding angles are angles in matching positions at the two crossings. Alternate interior angles are on opposite sides of the transversal, between the two lines. Alternate exterior angles are on opposite sides, outside the two lines. Co-interior (or interior) angles are on the same side of the transversal, between the lines. Knowing the names of these angle pairs is important for studying the special angles formed when a transversal cuts parallel lines.
topics.easyExplanation: A transversal is a line that cuts two or more lines. It forms pairs of angles: corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior and co-interior angles.

topics.topic: Angles Made by a Transversal on Parallel Lines
topics.meaning: When a transversal cuts parallel lines, corresponding and alternate angles are equal, and co-interior angles are supplementary.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a transversal crosses a pair of parallel lines, special angle relationships appear. Corresponding angles (in matching positions) are equal. Alternate interior angles (on opposite sides of the transversal, between the lines) are equal, and alternate exterior angles are also equal. Co-interior angles (on the same side of the transversal, between the lines) are supplementary, adding up to 180 degrees. These properties let us find unknown angles when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and they can also be used in reverse to check whether two lines are parallel.

topics.meaning: A triangle is a closed figure made of three sides, and it has three sides, three angles and three vertices.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A triangle is the simplest closed figure made of straight lines; it is formed by three line segments. Every triangle has three elements of each kind: three sides, three angles (at the corners) and three vertices (corners). The three sides and three angles together make up the six elements of a triangle. Triangles are named by their vertices, for example triangle ABC. The triangle is a very important shape in geometry and in real life, because it is strong and is used in bridges, roofs and many structures. Studying the parts of a triangle is the first step to learning its many properties.

topics.meaning: A median of a triangle is a line segment joining a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A median of a triangle is a special line segment that joins a vertex (corner) of the triangle to the midpoint (the exact middle) of the opposite side. Since a triangle has three vertices, it has three medians, one from each vertex. An interesting property is that all three medians of a triangle pass through a single point, called the centroid. The medians divide the triangle into smaller parts of equal area. Medians help us understand the balance and structure of a triangle and are used in more advanced geometry. Drawing the medians is a good way to explore the triangle's properties.

topics.meaning: An altitude of a triangle is the perpendicular segment from a vertex to the opposite side.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An altitude of a triangle is the perpendicular line segment drawn from a vertex to the opposite side (or the line containing it). The altitude makes a right angle with the side it falls on, and it represents the 'height' of the triangle from that side. Since a triangle has three vertices, it has three altitudes, one from each vertex. The three altitudes (or their extensions) also pass through a single point. The altitude is especially important because it is used as the height when finding the area of a triangle. Knowing the altitudes helps us understand the height and the area of a triangle.

topics.topic: Angle Sum Property of a Triangle
topics.meaning: The three interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180 degrees.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The angle sum property is one of the most important properties of triangles. It states that the sum of the three interior angles of any triangle is always 180 degrees, no matter what kind of triangle it is. This means that if we know two of the angles of a triangle, we can find the third by subtracting their sum from 180 degrees. For example, if two angles are 60 degrees and 70 degrees, the third angle is 180 - (60 + 70) = 50 degrees. We can check this property by tearing off the three corners of a paper triangle and placing them together; they form a straight angle of 180 degrees. This property is used to find unknown angles in many problems.
topics.easyExplanation: The three angles of any triangle always add up to 180 degrees. If two angles are known, the third is 180 minus their sum.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If two angles are known, the third is 180 minus their sum.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example

topics.meaning: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two interior opposite angles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: If one side of a triangle is extended beyond a vertex, the angle formed between this extended side and the next side of the triangle is called an exterior angle of the triangle. The exterior angle property states that an exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles (the two angles inside the triangle that are not next to the exterior angle). For example, if the two interior opposite angles are 60 degrees and 50 degrees, the exterior angle is 60 + 50 = 110 degrees. This property follows from the angle sum property and is very useful for finding unknown angles in triangles.

topics.meaning: Triangles are classified by their sides as equilateral, isosceles or scalene, and by their angles as acute, right or obtuse.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Triangles can be classified in two ways. By their sides: an equilateral triangle has all three sides equal (and all angles 60 degrees), an isosceles triangle has two sides equal (and the angles opposite them equal), and a scalene triangle has all three sides of different lengths. By their angles: an acute triangle has all three angles less than 90 degrees, a right triangle has one angle of exactly 90 degrees, and an obtuse triangle has one angle greater than 90 degrees. So a triangle has both a 'side' name and an 'angle' name. Classifying triangles helps us describe them and use their special properties.

topics.meaning: In any triangle, the sum of the lengths of any two sides is greater than the length of the third side.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The triangle inequality is an important property that tells us when three lengths can form a triangle. It states that the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the length of the third side. This must be true for all three pairs of sides. For example, sides of 5 cm, 6 cm and 8 cm can form a triangle because 5 + 6 = 11 is greater than 8, and the same is true for the other pairs. But sides of 3 cm, 4 cm and 8 cm cannot form a triangle, because 3 + 4 = 7 is not greater than 8. So before constructing a triangle, we can use this rule to check whether the given lengths are possible.
topics.easyExplanation: In any triangle, any two sides add to more than the third side. So 5, 6, 8 can form a triangle, but 3, 4, 8 cannot (3 + 4 is not more than 8).

topics.meaning: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Pythagoras theorem is an important property of right-angled triangles (triangles with one 90-degree angle). The side opposite the right angle is the longest side and is called the hypotenuse. The theorem states that the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. If the two shorter sides are a and b and the hypotenuse is c, then a-squared + b-squared = c-squared. For example, in a triangle with sides 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is 5 because 9 + 16 = 25. This theorem is used to find an unknown side of a right triangle and to check whether a triangle is right-angled.
topics.easyExplanation: In a right triangle, (hypotenuse)^2 = (side1)^2 + (side2)^2. For sides 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is 5, since 9 + 16 = 25.

topics.meaning: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two figures are said to be congruent if they are exactly the same in both shape and size, so that one can be placed exactly over the other and they cover each other completely. Congruent figures are like exact copies of each other; if we cut out one, it will fit perfectly on top of the other. For example, two stamps of the same design, or two coins of the same value, are congruent. Congruence is different from just being similar in shape, because congruent figures must also be the same size. The symbol for congruence is a special equals sign with a curve on top. Understanding congruence helps us compare shapes precisely.
topics.easyExplanation: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size, so one fits exactly over the other, like two identical stamps.

topics.topic: Congruence of Line Segments and Angles
topics.meaning: Two line segments are congruent if they have the same length, and two angles are congruent if they have the same measure.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Before studying congruent triangles, we look at congruence of simpler figures. Two line segments are congruent if and only if they have the same length; for example, two segments each 5 cm long are congruent. Two angles are congruent if and only if they have the same measure; for example, two angles each of 40 degrees are congruent. Note that congruent angles need only be equal in measure; the length of their arms does not matter. These simple cases of congruence prepare us to understand the congruence of triangles, where corresponding sides and corresponding angles must match.
topics.easyExplanation: Two line segments are congruent if they have the same length; two angles are congruent if they have the same measure (arm length does not matter).

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two line segments are congruent if they have the same length.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two segments each 5 cm long are congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Congruent angles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two angles are congruent if they have the same measure.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Arm length not counted
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For angles, only the measure matters, not the arm length.

Two angles each of 40 degrees are congruent.

importantKeywords.definition: The size of an angle in degrees.

topics.quickSummary: Two line segments are congruent if they have the same length, and two angles are congruent if they have the same measure, regardless of the length of their arms.
revisionNotes: Arm length does not affect angle congruence.

topics.meaning: Two triangles are congruent if their corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two triangles are congruent if one can be placed exactly over the other so that they cover each other completely. When two triangles are congruent, their corresponding parts match exactly: their corresponding sides are equal and their corresponding angles are equal. 'Corresponding' means the parts that are in matching positions in the two triangles. To write that two triangles are congruent, we must match the vertices in the correct order, for example triangle ABC is congruent to triangle PQR means A matches P, B matches Q and C matches R. We do not need to check all six parts, though; there are shortcuts called congruence criteria.
topics.easyExplanation: Two triangles are congruent if their corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal. We match the vertices in the correct order.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Triangles fit exactly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Congruent triangles can be placed exactly over each other.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Corresponding parts equal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Their corresponding sides and angles are all equal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Matching positions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 'Corresponding' means parts in matching positions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Correct order
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We write the vertices in the correct matching order.

examples: If triangle ABC is congruent to triangle PQR, then AB = PQ, and so on.
Congruent triangles have equal corresponding sides and angles.

importantKeywords.definition: Triangles with the same shape and size.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Two triangles that fit exactly are congruent.

topics.quickSummary: Two triangles are congruent if their corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal; we match the vertices in the correct order, and shortcuts (criteria) avoid checking all six parts.
revisionNotes: Congruent triangles fit exactly over each other.
Corresponding sides and angles are equal.
Match vertices in the correct order.
Criteria avoid checking all six parts.

topics.meaning: By the SSS criterion, two triangles are congruent if the three sides of one equal the three sides of the other.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We do not need to check all six parts of two triangles to prove they are congruent; certain sets of three matching parts are enough. The SSS (Side-Side-Side) criterion states that if the three sides of one triangle are equal to the three sides of another triangle, then the two triangles are congruent. This is because three given side lengths fix the exact shape and size of a triangle, so two triangles with the same three sides must be identical. For example, if both triangles have sides 5 cm, 6 cm and 7 cm, they are congruent by SSS. This criterion is very useful and simple to apply when we know all three sides.
topics.easyExplanation: By SSS, if the three sides of one triangle equal the three sides of another, the triangles are congruent. Three sides fix a triangle's shape and size.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three sides enough
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We need only matching parts, not all six, to prove congruence.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The SSS rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If three sides of one triangle equal three sides of another, they are congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why it works
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Three side lengths fix the exact shape and size of a triangle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Triangles with sides 5, 6, 7 are congruent by SSS.

examples: Two triangles with all three sides equal are congruent by SSS.
Sides 5, 6 and 7 fix the triangle's shape.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: SSS proves congruence with three sides.
SSS shows two triangles are congruent.

topics.quickSummary: By the SSS (Side-Side-Side) criterion, two triangles are congruent if the three sides of one equal the three sides of the other, because three sides fix a triangle's shape and size.
revisionNotes: SSS = three equal sides prove congruence.
Simple when all three sides are known.

topics.meaning: By the SAS criterion, two triangles are congruent if two sides and the angle between them are equal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The SAS (Side-Angle-Side) criterion is another shortcut for proving congruence. It states that if two sides and the angle between them (the included angle) of one triangle are equal to two sides and the included angle of another triangle, then the two triangles are congruent. It is important that the angle is the one between the two given sides; this is why it is called the 'included' angle. For example, if both triangles have two sides of 5 cm and 7 cm with an included angle of 50 degrees, they are congruent by SAS. The SAS criterion is very commonly used when we know two sides and the angle between them.
topics.easyExplanation: By SAS, if two sides and the angle between them in one triangle equal those in another, the triangles are congruent. The angle must be between the two sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The SAS rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two sides and the included angle equal makes triangles congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Included angle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The angle must be the one between the two given sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why it works
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two sides and the angle between them fix the triangle's shape.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sides 5 and 7 with included angle 50 give congruence by SAS.

examples: Two triangles with two sides and the included angle equal are congruent by SAS.
The angle must be between the two given sides.

importantKeywords.definition: Congruence by two sides and the included angle.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: SAS uses two sides and the angle between.

importantKeywords.definition: The angle between two given sides.

topics.quickSummary: By the SAS (Side-Angle-Side) criterion, two triangles are congruent if two sides and the included angle (between them) of one equal those of the other.
revisionNotes: SAS = two sides + included angle equal.
The angle must be between the two sides.

topics.meaning: By the ASA criterion, two triangles are congruent if two angles and the side between them are equal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) criterion is another way to prove triangles congruent. It states that if two angles and the side between them (the included side) of one triangle are equal to two angles and the included side of another triangle, then the two triangles are congruent. The side must be the one between the two given angles. For example, if both triangles have two angles of 50 degrees and 60 degrees with the included side of 6 cm equal, they are congruent by ASA. Since the three angles of a triangle add to 180 degrees, knowing two angles also fixes the third. The ASA criterion is used when we know two angles and the side between them.
topics.easyExplanation: By ASA, if two angles and the side between them in one triangle equal those in another, the triangles are congruent. The side must be between the two angles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The ASA rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two angles and the included side equal makes triangles congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Included side
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The side must be the one between the two given angles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Third angle fixed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Knowing two angles fixes the third (angles add to 180).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Angles 50 and 60 with included side 6 give congruence by ASA.

examples: Two triangles with two angles and the included side equal are congruent by ASA.
The side must be between the two given angles.

importantKeywords.definition: Congruence by two angles and the included side.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: ASA uses two angles and the side between.

importantKeywords.definition: The side between two given angles.
A corner measure of a triangle.

topics.quickSummary: By the ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) criterion, two triangles are congruent if two angles and the included side (between them) of one equal those of the other.
revisionNotes: ASA = two angles + included side equal.
The side must be between the two angles.

topics.meaning: By the RHS criterion, two right triangles are congruent if their hypotenuse and one side are equal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) criterion applies only to right-angled triangles. It states that if the hypotenuse and one side of one right triangle are equal to the hypotenuse and one side of another right triangle, then the two triangles are congruent. Both triangles must have a right angle. This criterion is useful because, in a right triangle, knowing the hypotenuse and one other side fixes the whole triangle (the third side can be found by the Pythagoras theorem). For example, two right triangles with hypotenuse 10 cm and one side 6 cm are congruent by RHS. RHS is a special and very handy criterion for right-angled triangles.
topics.easyExplanation: By RHS, two right triangles are congruent if their hypotenuse and one side are equal. Both must have a right angle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Only right triangles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The RHS criterion applies only to right-angled triangles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The RHS rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equal hypotenuse and one side make two right triangles congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Both have a right angle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each triangle must have a right angle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why it works
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Hypotenuse and one side fix the whole right triangle.

examples: Two right triangles with equal hypotenuse and one side are congruent by RHS.
RHS only works for right-angled triangles.

importantKeywords.definition: Congruence of right triangles by hypotenuse and a side.
The longest side of a right triangle.

topics.quickSummary: By the RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) criterion, two right triangles are congruent if their hypotenuse and one side are equal, since these fix the whole right triangle.
revisionNotes: RHS = right triangle, hypotenuse + a side equal.
Both must have a right angle.

topics.topic: Why AAA Does Not Prove Congruence
topics.meaning: Having all three angles equal (AAA) makes triangles the same shape but not necessarily the same size, so it does not prove congruence.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: It might seem that if two triangles have all three angles equal (AAA), they should be congruent, but this is not true. Two triangles can have exactly the same three angles and still be of different sizes; for example, a small equilateral triangle and a large equilateral triangle both have all angles of 60 degrees, but they are clearly not congruent because one is bigger than the other. When two triangles have equal angles but different sizes, they are called similar, not congruent. This is why AAA only proves similarity (same shape) and not congruence (same shape and size). To prove congruence, we must use SSS, SAS, ASA or RHS, which all involve at least one side.
topics.easyExplanation: AAA (all angles equal) only makes triangles the same shape, not the same size, so it does not prove congruence. We need SSS, SAS, ASA or RHS.

stepByStepExplanation.title: AAA seems enough
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It might seem all angles equal proves congruence, but it does not.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Different sizes possible
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Triangles with the same angles can be different sizes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Similar, not congruent
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equal angles but different sizes means similar, not congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Need a side
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To prove congruence, use SSS, SAS, ASA or RHS, which involve a side.

examples: A small and a large equilateral triangle both have 60-degree angles but are not congruent.
Equal angles only make triangles similar in shape.

importantKeywords.definition: Same shape but possibly different size.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Congruence needs a side, not just angles.

topics.quickSummary: Having all three angles equal (AAA) makes triangles similar (same shape) but not necessarily congruent (same size), so congruence must be proven using SSS, SAS, ASA or RHS.
revisionNotes: AAA only makes triangles the same shape.

topics.meaning: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A ratio is a way of comparing two quantities of the same kind by division. The ratio of a quantity a to a quantity b is written as a : b, read as 'a is to b'. For example, if there are 4 boys and 6 girls, the ratio of boys to girls is 4 : 6, which can be simplified to 2 : 3 by dividing both numbers by 2. A ratio has no units, because the two quantities must be in the same unit before comparing. Ratios are used to compare amounts, such as the ratio of ingredients in a recipe. We usually write a ratio in its simplest form.
topics.easyExplanation: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division, written a : b. The ratio of 4 boys to 6 girls is 4 : 6 = 2 : 3.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A ratio is written in its simplest form, like 4 : 6 = 2 : 3.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The two quantities must be in the same unit; a ratio has no units.

importantKeywords.definition: A comparison of two quantities by division.
To see how two amounts relate.

topics.quickSummary: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division, written a : b; the quantities must be in the same unit, and a ratio is usually written in its simplest form.
revisionNotes: Same unit; a ratio has no units.

topics.meaning: When two ratios are equal, they form a proportion, and equal ratios are called equivalent ratios.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two ratios that are equal are said to be in proportion. For example, the ratios 2 : 3 and 4 : 6 are equal (both simplify to 2 : 3), so they are in proportion, written 2 : 3 = 4 : 6 or 2 : 3 :: 4 : 6. Equal ratios are also called equivalent ratios, just as we have equivalent fractions. We can make equivalent ratios by multiplying or dividing both parts of a ratio by the same number. To check whether four numbers are in proportion, we see whether the two ratios are equal. Proportion is useful in solving many problems, especially using the unitary method.
topics.easyExplanation: When two ratios are equal, they are in proportion (2 : 3 = 4 : 6). Equal ratios are equivalent ratios, made by multiplying or dividing both parts equally.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two ratios that are equal are in proportion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example

stepByStepExplanation.title: Equivalent ratios
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equal ratios are called equivalent ratios.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Making them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply or divide both parts of a ratio by the same number.

Multiplying 2 : 3 by 2 gives the equivalent ratio 4 : 6.

topics.meaning: The unitary method finds the value of one unit first, and then the value of the required number of units.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The unitary method is a useful way to solve problems about quantities and prices. In this method, we first find the value of one unit, and then multiply to find the value of the required number of units. For example, if 5 pens cost 50 rupees, then one pen costs 50 divided by 5 = 10 rupees, so 8 pens cost 8 multiplied by 10 = 80 rupees. The unitary method works because the quantities are in proportion. It is widely used in everyday life for shopping, comparing prices, and solving problems about speed, work and many other situations. The key step is always to find the value of one unit first.
topics.easyExplanation: The unitary method finds the value of one unit first, then multiplies for the needed number. If 5 pens cost 50, one pen costs 10, so 8 pens cost 80.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find one unit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: First find the value of one unit.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Then multiply
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply to find the value of the required number of units.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 5 pens cost 50, so one pen costs 10, and 8 pens cost 80.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Based on proportion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The unitary method works because quantities are in proportion.

examples: If 5 pens cost 50 rupees, 8 pens cost 80 rupees.

topics.meaning: A percentage is a way of comparing a quantity to 100; 'per cent' means 'out of 100'.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A percentage is a special kind of ratio that compares a quantity to 100. The words 'per cent' mean 'out of 100', and the symbol for per cent is %. So 50% means 50 out of 100, which is the same as the fraction 50/100 or 1/2. Percentages are useful because comparing everything to 100 makes it easy to compare different quantities. For example, marks, discounts and results are often given as percentages. To find a percentage of a quantity, we multiply; for example, 20% of 50 = (20/100) x 50 = 10. Percentages are used everywhere in daily life.
topics.easyExplanation: A percentage compares a quantity to 100; % means 'out of 100'. So 50% = 50/100 = 1/2, and 20% of 50 = 10.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What a percentage is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A percentage compares a quantity to 100.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Per cent means out of 100
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 50% means 50 out of 100, the same as 50/100 or 1/2.
