topics.topic: Converting Between Fractions, Decimals and Percentages
topics.meaning: Fractions, decimals and percentages can be converted from one to another.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fractions, decimals and percentages are three different ways of writing the same value, and we can convert between them. To change a fraction or decimal to a percentage, we multiply by 100 and add the % sign; for example, 1/2 = 0.5, and 0.5 x 100 = 50%. To change a percentage to a fraction, we write it over 100 and simplify; for example, 25% = 25/100 = 1/4. To change a percentage to a decimal, we divide by 100; for example, 25% = 0.25. Being able to convert between these forms is very useful, because some problems are easier to solve in one form than another.
topics.easyExplanation: Fractions, decimals and percentages are different ways to write the same value. 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%. Multiply by 100 to get a percentage; divide by 100 to undo it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three forms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fractions, decimals and percentages can all show the same value.

stepByStepExplanation.title: To a percentage
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply a fraction or decimal by 100 and add %.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Percentage to fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the percentage over 100 and simplify.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Percentage to decimal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide the percentage by 100.

topics.meaning: Percentages are used to describe and calculate increases and decreases in quantities.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Percentages are very useful for describing how much a quantity has changed. A percentage increase tells us how much a quantity has grown, as a percentage of the original; a percentage decrease tells us how much it has fallen. To find a percentage increase or decrease, we find the change, divide it by the original amount, and multiply by 100. For example, if a price rises from 100 rupees to 120 rupees, the increase is 20 rupees, and the percentage increase is (20/100) x 100 = 20%. Percentages are used for discounts, price rises, population changes and many other real-life situations where we compare a change to the original amount.
topics.easyExplanation: Percentages describe increases and decreases: change ÷ original x 100. A price rising from 100 to 120 is a 20% increase.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Percentages describe how much a quantity has increased or decreased.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the change, divide by the original, and multiply by 100.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A price rising from 100 to 120 is a 20% increase.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Used for discounts, price rises and population changes.

topics.meaning: Profit or loss is found by comparing the cost price and selling price, often as a percentage.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When something is bought and sold, we compare its cost price (CP), the price at which it is bought, with its selling price (SP), the price at which it is sold. If the selling price is more than the cost price, there is a profit (gain); if it is less, there is a loss. Profit = SP - CP, and Loss = CP - SP. Profit or loss is often expressed as a percentage of the cost price: Profit% = (Profit / CP) x 100, and Loss% = (Loss / CP) x 100. For example, buying for 80 and selling for 100 gives a profit of 20, and a profit percentage of (20/80) x 100 = 25%. These ideas are used in everyday buying and selling.
topics.easyExplanation: Profit happens when SP > CP; loss when SP < CP. Profit = SP - CP; Profit% = (Profit/CP) x 100. Buying at 80, selling at 100 gives 25% profit.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If SP > CP there is profit; if SP < CP there is loss.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Calculate them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Profit = SP - CP; Loss = CP - SP.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Profit% = (Profit / CP) x 100; Loss% = (Loss / CP) x 100.

If CP = 80 and profit = 20, profit% = 25%.

topics.meaning: Simple interest is the extra money paid for borrowing or earned for lending, calculated on the principal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we borrow money or deposit money in a bank, we pay or earn extra money called interest. The money borrowed or deposited is called the principal (P). The interest is usually charged at a certain rate per cent per year, called the rate (R), for a certain time in years (T). Simple interest is calculated using the formula: SI = (P x R x T) / 100. The total amount to be paid back is the Amount = Principal + Simple Interest. For example, the simple interest on 1000 rupees at 10% for 2 years is (1000 x 10 x 2)/100 = 200 rupees, and the amount is 1200 rupees. Simple interest is used in everyday money matters.
topics.easyExplanation: Simple interest is the extra money on a loan or deposit: SI = (P x R x T)/100. The total to repay is Amount = Principal + Interest.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Amount = Principal + Simple Interest.

topics.meaning: Rational numbers are needed to write numbers like fractions and negative fractions that whole numbers and integers cannot show.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We have learnt about natural numbers, whole numbers and integers. But these are not enough to describe every quantity. For example, we cannot show 'half of something' using only integers, and we cannot show 'minus three-quarters' using only fractions of whole numbers. To handle parts of a whole, including negative parts, we need a larger set of numbers called rational numbers. Rational numbers include all integers, all fractions, and their negatives, so they let us write numbers like 1/2, -3/4 and -5. The need to measure, share and describe quantities that are not whole, both positive and negative, is why rational numbers are important.
topics.easyExplanation: Integers cannot show parts of a whole like one-half or minus three-quarters. Rational numbers are a larger set that includes fractions and their negatives.

topics.meaning: A rational number is a number that can be written in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q is not zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A rational number is any number that can be written in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q (the denominator) is not zero. The number p is the numerator and q is the denominator. This definition includes many kinds of numbers: every integer is a rational number (for example, 3 = 3/1), every fraction is a rational number, and so are their negatives, like -3/4. So numbers like 3, -2, 1/2, -5/7 and 0 are all rational numbers. The only rule is that the denominator cannot be zero, because division by zero is not allowed. Rational numbers can be positive, negative or zero.
topics.easyExplanation: A rational number can be written as p/q (q not zero), like 1/2, -3/4 or 3. Integers, fractions and their negatives are all rational numbers.

topics.meaning: A rational number is positive if its numerator and denominator have the same sign, and negative if they have different signs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Rational numbers can be positive or negative. A rational number is positive if both its numerator and denominator have the same sign (both positive or both negative); for example, 3/4 and (-3)/(-4) are positive. A rational number is negative if its numerator and denominator have different signs (one positive and one negative); for example, -3/4 and 3/(-4) are negative. The number 0 is a rational number that is neither positive nor negative. On the number line, positive rational numbers lie to the right of zero and negative rational numbers lie to the left, just like integers. Knowing the sign of a rational number helps us compare and order them.
topics.easyExplanation: A rational number is positive if its numerator and denominator have the same sign, and negative if they have different signs. Zero is neither.

topics.topic: Rational Numbers on the Number Line
topics.meaning: Rational numbers can be shown on the number line by dividing the space between integers into equal parts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Just like integers and fractions, rational numbers can be represented on the number line. To mark a rational number such as 3/4, we divide the space between two integers (here 0 and 1) into as many equal parts as the denominator (4 parts) and count the number given by the numerator (3 parts) from zero. Negative rational numbers are marked to the left of zero in the same way; for example, -3/4 is three of four parts to the left of zero. On the number line, a rational number further to the right is greater. There are infinitely many rational numbers between any two rational numbers. Showing rational numbers on the line helps us compare and understand them.

topics.topic: Standard Form of a Rational Number
topics.meaning: A rational number is in standard form when its denominator is positive and the numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A rational number can be written in many equivalent forms, but it has one special form called the standard form (or simplest form). A rational number is in standard form when two conditions are met: its denominator is a positive integer, and the numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1 (they are co-prime). To write a rational number in standard form, we first make the denominator positive (if needed) and then divide the numerator and denominator by their highest common factor. For example, -6/8 in standard form is -3/4, and 4/(-6) becomes -4/6 = -2/3. Writing rational numbers in standard form makes them easy to compare and work with.

topics.meaning: Rational numbers are compared by writing them with the same positive denominator or by using the number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To compare two rational numbers and find which is greater, we can write them with the same positive denominator and then compare the numerators; the one with the larger numerator is greater. We can also compare them using a number line, where a number to the right is greater. Some helpful facts make comparing easier: every positive rational number is greater than zero, and every negative rational number is less than zero, so positive rational numbers are always greater than negative ones. Among two negative rational numbers, the one closer to zero is greater. Comparing rational numbers lets us arrange them in order.

topics.topic: Addition and Subtraction of Rational Numbers
topics.meaning: Rational numbers are added or subtracted by giving them a common denominator and then adding or subtracting the numerators.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To add or subtract rational numbers, we use the same idea as for fractions, while also following the sign rules for integers. First, we write the rational numbers with the same denominator (a common denominator). Then we add or subtract the numerators and keep the common denominator. For example, (2/3) + (1/3) = 3/3 = 1, and (1/2) + (-1/3) = 3/6 + (-2/6) = 1/6. To subtract a rational number, we can add its additive inverse (its opposite). The additive inverse of a/b is -a/b. The final answer is usually written in standard form. These rules let us add and subtract all rational numbers, positive and negative.

topics.topic: Multiplication and Division of Rational Numbers
topics.meaning: Rational numbers are multiplied by multiplying numerators and denominators, and divided by multiplying by the reciprocal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply two rational numbers, we multiply the numerators together and the denominators together, following the sign rules; for example, (2/3) x (-4/5) = -8/15. To divide one rational number by another, we multiply by the reciprocal of the second; the reciprocal of a/b is b/a. For example, (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 10/12 = 5/6. The sign rules are the same as for integers: like signs give a positive result and unlike signs give a negative result. The final answer is written in standard form. With these rules, we can carry out all four operations on rational numbers.

topics.meaning: Geometric constructions are done using a ruler, a compass, a divider, set squares and a protractor.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Practical geometry is about drawing accurate figures using special tools, found in a geometry box. The ruler (or straightedge) is used to draw straight lines and to measure lengths. The compass is used to draw circles and arcs and to mark off equal lengths. The divider is used to compare and measure lengths. The set squares are used to draw perpendicular and parallel lines. The protractor is used to measure and draw angles. Using these tools correctly and carefully lets us make precise constructions. Learning to use the tools is the first step to doing accurate geometric constructions.

topics.meaning: Using a ruler and compass, we can construct a line parallel to a given line through a point not on it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: One important construction is drawing a line parallel to a given line through a point outside it. The method uses the property that when a transversal cuts two lines making equal alternate (or corresponding) angles, the two lines are parallel. We draw a transversal from the given point to the given line, then use the compass to copy the angle at the new point so that the alternate angles are equal, and draw the line through the new point. This line will be parallel to the given line. This construction relies on the angle properties of parallel lines and gives an exact parallel line without using a protractor.

topics.meaning: Using a ruler and compass, we can construct a triangle when the lengths of its three sides are given.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When the three sides of a triangle are given, we can construct it accurately using a ruler and compass, based on the SSS condition. The steps are: first, draw one side of the given length with a ruler. Then, from one endpoint, use the compass to draw an arc with a radius equal to the second side, and from the other endpoint, draw an arc with a radius equal to the third side. The point where the two arcs cross is the third vertex. Finally, join this vertex to the two endpoints to complete the triangle. The triangle so drawn has exactly the three given sides. This construction shows how three sides uniquely fix a triangle.

Then, along this new arm, measure and mark the length of the second given side with a ruler or compass, to get the third vertex. Finally, join the third vertex to the other endpoint of the first side to complete the triangle. The triangle drawn has exactly the two given sides and the included angle, showing how SAS fixes a triangle.

topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we are given the hypotenuse and one side of a right-angled triangle, we can construct it using the RHS condition. The steps are: first, draw the given side with a ruler. At one endpoint of this side, draw a right angle (90 degrees) using a protractor or set square. topics.topic: Copying a Line Segment and an Angle
topics.meaning: Using a compass, we can copy a line segment of a given length and an angle of a given measure.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two basic constructions used in geometry are copying a line segment and copying an angle. To copy a line segment, we open the compass to the length of the given segment, then draw a line and mark off the same length on it, giving an exact copy. To copy an angle, we draw an arc across the given angle, then draw the same arc on a new line and use the compass to transfer the width of the angle, so the new angle is exactly equal to the given one. These copying constructions are important because they are used as steps within larger constructions, such as drawing parallel lines and triangles. They show how a compass lets us transfer lengths and angles accurately.

topics.meaning: Each construction condition (SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS) gives exactly one triangle, showing the construction is unique.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An important idea in practical geometry is that each set of given measurements fixes exactly one triangle, so the construction is unique. When three sides are given (SSS), or two sides and the included angle (SAS), or two angles and the included side (ASA), or the hypotenuse and a side of a right triangle (RHS), there is only one triangle (up to congruence) that can be drawn with those measurements. This is the same idea as the congruence criteria: the conditions that let us construct a unique triangle are exactly the conditions that prove two triangles congruent. However, if only the three angles are given (AAA), the triangle is not unique, because triangles of different sizes can have the same angles.

topics.meaning: Perimeter is the total length of the boundary of a closed flat shape.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The perimeter of a closed flat shape is the total distance around its boundary, found by adding the lengths of all its sides. For a rectangle with length l and breadth b, since opposite sides are equal, the perimeter is 2 x (l + b). For a square with side s, since all four sides are equal, the perimeter is 4 x s. Perimeter is measured in units of length, like centimetres or metres. Perimeter is useful in real life, for example to find how much fencing is needed around a field or how much lace is needed to border a cloth. The key idea is that perimeter is a length, found by adding up all the sides.

topics.meaning: Area is the amount of flat surface a shape covers, measured in square units.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The area of a flat shape is the amount of surface it covers, measured in square units like square centimetres or square metres. For a rectangle, the area is length multiplied by breadth (l x b). For a square, the area is side multiplied by side (s x s). On squared paper, we can find the area of a shape by counting the unit squares it covers, counting full squares as one and pairing up half squares. Area is useful for finding how much carpet covers a floor or how much paint covers a wall. The key idea is that area is a surface, measured in square units, while perimeter is a length.
topics.easyExplanation: Area is the surface a shape covers, in square units. Rectangle: length x breadth; square: side x side.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Area is the amount of flat surface a shape covers.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Area of a rectangle = length x breadth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Square rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Area of a square = side x side; on grid paper, count unit squares.

examples: A rectangle 5 cm by 3 cm has area 5 x 3 = 15 square cm.
A square of side 4 cm has area 4 x 4 = 16 square cm.

topics.meaning: The area of a parallelogram is its base multiplied by its height.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A parallelogram is a four-sided shape with opposite sides parallel and equal. To find the area of a parallelogram, we use its base and its height. The base is any one of its sides, and the height is the perpendicular distance between that base and the opposite side. The area of a parallelogram is given by the formula: Area = base x height. It is important that the height is the perpendicular distance, not the slanting side. We can understand this formula by cutting a triangle from one end of a parallelogram and moving it to the other end to form a rectangle. The area of a parallelogram is useful for finding the area of slanted shapes.
topics.easyExplanation: The area of a parallelogram = base x height, where the height is the perpendicular distance between the base and the opposite side.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A parallelogram has opposite sides parallel and equal.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The base is a side; the height is the perpendicular distance to the opposite side.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Area of a parallelogram = base x height.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The height must be perpendicular, not the slanting side.

examples: A parallelogram with base 6 cm and height 4 cm has area 24 square cm.

topics.meaning: The area of a triangle is half of its base multiplied by its height.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A triangle is half of a parallelogram (or a rectangle), so its area is half of the base times the height. The area of a triangle is given by the formula: Area = (1/2) x base x height. The base is any one side of the triangle, and the height is the perpendicular distance (the altitude) from that base to the opposite vertex. We can understand this formula because two equal triangles can be put together to form a parallelogram, whose area is base x height, so one triangle has half that area. The area of a triangle is very useful, because many shapes can be divided into triangles to find their areas.
topics.easyExplanation: The area of a triangle = (1/2) x base x height, since a triangle is half of a parallelogram. The height is the perpendicular distance from the base.

topics.meaning: The circumference of a circle is the distance around it, given by 2 x pi x radius.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A circle is a round closed figure, and the distance around it is called its circumference (which is like the perimeter of a circle). The distance from the centre of the circle to any point on it is the radius (r), and the distance right across the circle through the centre is the diameter (d), which is twice the radius (d = 2r). The circumference of a circle is given by the formula C = 2 x pi x r, or C = pi x d, where pi is a special number approximately equal to 22/7 or 3.14. The ratio of the circumference to the diameter of any circle is always pi. The circumference is used to find the distance around circular objects like wheels and plates.
topics.easyExplanation: The circumference (distance around a circle) is 2 x pi x r, where pi is about 22/7 or 3.14 and r is the radius. Diameter = 2 x radius.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What circumference is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The circumference is the distance around a circle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Radius and diameter
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The radius is centre to edge; the diameter is across through the centre (d = 2r).

stepByStepExplanation.title: The formula
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Circumference C = 2 x pi x r (or pi x d).

stepByStepExplanation.title: The number pi
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pi is about 22/7 or 3.14; circumference ÷ diameter is always pi.

examples: A circle of radius 7 cm has circumference 2 x (22/7) x 7 = 44 cm.
The circumference is the distance around a wheel.

topics.meaning: The area of a circle is given by pi multiplied by the radius squared.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The area of a circle is the amount of surface enclosed inside it. The area of a circle is given by the formula: Area = pi x r x r (pi times the radius squared), where r is the radius and pi is about 22/7 or 3.14. For example, a circle of radius 7 cm has area (22/7) x 7 x 7 = 154 square cm. The area is measured in square units. We can understand this formula by imagining the circle cut into many thin sectors and rearranged to form a shape close to a rectangle. The area of a circle is useful for finding the surface of circular objects, like the top of a table or a coin.
topics.easyExplanation: The area of a circle = pi x r x r (pi times radius squared), where pi is about 22/7 or 3.14. A circle of radius 7 has area 154 square cm.

topics.meaning: Units of area are converted using the squares of the length conversions, like 1 square metre = 10,000 square centimetres.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Just as we convert units of length, we sometimes need to convert units of area. Because area involves multiplying two lengths, the conversion factor for area is the square of the conversion factor for length. For example, since 1 metre = 100 centimetres, 1 square metre = 100 x 100 = 10,000 square centimetres. Similarly, 1 square kilometre = 1000 x 1000 = 1,000,000 square metres, and a hectare (used to measure land) is 10,000 square metres. Knowing how to convert area units is important when comparing areas given in different units, such as a room measured in square metres and a tile measured in square centimetres. The key is to square the length conversion.
topics.easyExplanation: Area units convert using the square of length conversions: 1 square metre = 100 x 100 = 10,000 square centimetres.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why squaring
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Area is two lengths multiplied, so we square the length conversion.

topics.meaning: The area of a combined or irregular shape can be found by dividing it into simple shapes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many real shapes are not simple squares, rectangles, triangles or circles, but are made by combining these. To find the area of such a combined shape, we divide it into simple shapes whose areas we know how to find, work out the area of each part, and then add them together (or subtract, if a part is cut out). For example, the area of an L-shaped figure can be found by splitting it into two rectangles, or the area of a path around a garden can be found by subtracting the inner area from the outer area. This method of dividing into simple shapes lets us find the area of many real-life figures, such as floors, fields and gardens.
topics.easyExplanation: To find the area of a combined shape, split it into simple shapes (rectangles, triangles, circles), find each area, and add or subtract them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add or subtract
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the parts, or subtract a part that is cut out.

topics.meaning: Algebraic expressions are made of terms, which are products of factors, and the numerical factor of a term is its coefficient.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression is formed using variables (letters) and constants (numbers) joined by operations. An expression is made up of terms, which are separated by plus or minus signs; for example, in 3x + 5, the terms are 3x and 5. Each term is a product of factors; in the term 3x, the factors are 3 and x. The numerical factor of a term is called its coefficient; in 3x, the coefficient of x is 3. We can show the terms and factors of an expression using a 'tree' diagram. Understanding terms, factors and coefficients is the basis of working with algebraic expressions.
topics.easyExplanation: Expressions are made of terms (like 3x and 5), separated by + or -. Each term is a product of factors; the number in a term is its coefficient.

topics.meaning: Like terms have the same variables, while unlike terms have different variables.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The terms of an algebraic expression can be grouped into like terms and unlike terms. Like terms are terms that have exactly the same variables raised to the same powers; only their numerical coefficients may differ. For example, 3x and 5x are like terms, because both have the variable x. Unlike terms are terms that have different variables (or the same variable raised to different powers); for example, 3x and 5y are unlike terms, and so are 3x and 3x-squared. Only like terms can be added or subtracted together, which is why recognising like terms is important. Sorting terms into like and unlike is the first step before combining them.
topics.easyExplanation: Like terms have the same variables (3x and 5x); unlike terms have different variables (3x and 5y). Only like terms can be combined.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Only like combine

topics.meaning: Expressions are named by their number of terms as monomials, binomials, trinomials and polynomials.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Algebraic expressions are named according to how many terms they have. A monomial is an expression with just one term, like 3x or 5. A binomial is an expression with exactly two terms, like 3x + 5. A trinomial is an expression with exactly three terms, like x-squared + 2x + 1. An expression with one or more terms in general is called a polynomial. Knowing these names helps us describe and talk about expressions clearly. The number of terms is counted after combining any like terms, so an expression should be in its simplest form before we name it.
topics.easyExplanation: Expressions are named by their number of terms: monomial (1 term), binomial (2 terms), trinomial (3 terms); in general, polynomials.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Named by terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Expressions are named by how many terms they have.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Binomial and trinomial
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Binomial has two terms (3x + 5); trinomial has three.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An expression with one or more terms in general.

topics.meaning: Algebraic expressions are added by combining their like terms.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To add two or more algebraic expressions, we combine their like terms: we add the coefficients of the like terms and keep the variable part the same, while unlike terms are simply written as they are. For example, to add (2x + 3) and (4x + 1), we add the like terms 2x and 4x to get 6x, and add the constants 3 and 1 to get 4, giving 6x + 4. We can add expressions horizontally (in a row) or by writing like terms in columns and adding. The result is a simplified expression. Adding expressions is an important skill for working with algebra.
topics.easyExplanation: To add expressions, combine like terms: add their coefficients and keep the variable. So (2x + 3) + (4x + 1) = 6x + 4.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Adding expressions means combining their like terms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unlike terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Unlike terms are simply written as they are.

topics.meaning: Algebraic expressions are subtracted by changing the signs of the expression being subtracted and then combining like terms.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To subtract one algebraic expression from another, we change the sign of every term in the expression being subtracted, and then add the result, combining like terms. This is because subtracting an expression is the same as adding its opposite. For example, to subtract (2x + 1) from (5x + 3), we change the signs of 2x + 1 to get -2x - 1, and then add: (5x + 3) + (-2x - 1) = 3x + 2. We can do this in a row or by writing like terms in columns and changing signs before adding. Care with signs is important. Subtracting expressions is a key skill in algebra.
topics.easyExplanation: To subtract an expression, change the sign of each of its terms and then add, combining like terms. So (5x + 3) - (2x + 1) = 3x + 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Then add
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the result, combining like terms.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtracting an expression is the same as adding its opposite.

examples: Subtracting changes 2x + 1 to -2x - 1.

topics.topic: Finding the Value of an Expression
topics.meaning: The value of an algebraic expression is found by substituting given numbers for the variables.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression has different values depending on the value of its variable. To find the value of an expression, we substitute (put in) the given number in place of the variable and then calculate. For example, the value of 3x + 5 when x = 2 is 3 x 2 + 5 = 11, and when x = 0 it is 5. If an expression has more than one variable, we substitute a value for each. Care must be taken with the order of operations (multiply before adding). Finding the value of an expression is the link between algebra and real calculations, and it is the basis for using formulas.
topics.easyExplanation: To find an expression's value, put the given number in place of the variable and calculate. So 3x + 5 with x = 2 is 3 x 2 + 5 = 11.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Values change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An expression's value depends on the value of its variable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Calculate

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply before adding when calculating.

topics.meaning: Formulas are algebraic expressions used as rules to calculate quantities like perimeter, area and speed.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A formula is a rule written using variables that tells us how to calculate a quantity. Many of the rules we have learnt are formulas, for example the perimeter of a square is P = 4 x s, the area of a rectangle is A = l x b, and speed is s = d / t. To use a formula, we substitute the given values for the variables and calculate. For example, using P = 4 x s, the perimeter of a square of side 5 is 4 x 5 = 20. Formulas are very powerful because one formula can be used again and again for many different values. Writing rules as formulas is one of the most useful ideas in algebra.
topics.easyExplanation: A formula is a rule written with variables, like P = 4 x s for a square's perimeter. We substitute the values and calculate.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use it

topics.meaning: Algebraic expressions can be used to describe number patterns and general rules.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Algebra is very useful for describing patterns in a short, general way. Instead of writing out a long list of numbers, we can use a variable to write a single expression (a rule) that gives any term of the pattern. For example, the pattern of even numbers 2, 4, 6, 8, ... can be written as 2n, where n is the position (1st, 2nd, 3rd, ...); the pattern of odd numbers 1, 3, 5, ... can be written as 2n - 1. We can also use expressions to describe patterns made of dots or matchsticks, such as the number of matchsticks needed to make n squares in a row. Using algebra to describe patterns helps us find any term quickly and see the general rule.
topics.easyExplanation: Algebra describes number patterns with one expression. Even numbers are 2n; odd numbers are 2n - 1, where n is the position.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Expressions can describe dot or matchstick patterns too.

topics.quickSummary: Algebraic expressions can describe number patterns with a single general rule, like 2n for even numbers and 2n - 1 for odd numbers, letting us find any term quickly.

topics.meaning: An exponent shows how many times a number, called the base, is multiplied by itself.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Exponents give us a short way to write repeated multiplication. When a number is multiplied by itself several times, we write it using a base and an exponent (also called a power). For example, 2 x 2 x 2 is written as 2 with a small 3, read as 'two raised to the power three' or 'two cubed', and it equals 8. Here 2 is the base (the number being multiplied) and 3 is the exponent (how many times it is used). So an exponent is a shorthand: 5 to the power 4 means 5 x 5 x 5 x 5. Exponents make it much easier to write and work with repeated multiplication and very large numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: An exponent shows repeated multiplication: 2 to the power 3 means 2 x 2 x 2 = 8. The base is 2 (multiplied), the exponent is 3 (how many times).

topics.meaning: Large numbers can be written in a short exponent form, and read back as repeated multiplication.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Exponents let us write large numbers in a short form. For example, 10 multiplied by itself five times is 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 = 1,00,000, which we write as 10 to the power 5. So instead of writing many zeros, we can write a short exponent form. We can also write a number as a product of its prime factors using exponents; for example, 72 = 2 x 2 x 2 x 3 x 3 = 2 to the power 3 multiplied by 3 to the power 2. To read an exponent form, we expand it back into repeated multiplication. Being able to read and write numbers in exponent form makes working with large numbers much easier.
topics.easyExplanation: Large numbers can be written shortly with exponents: 10 to the power 5 = 1,00,000. Numbers can also be written as products of primes using exponents (72 = 2^3 x 3^2).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Short form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Exponents write large numbers in a short form.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 10 to the power 5 = 1,00,000 instead of writing many zeros.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prime factor form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Numbers can be written as products of primes using exponents, like 72 = 2^3 x 3^2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reading back
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To read an exponent form, expand it into repeated multiplication.

examples: 10 to the power 5 = 1,00,000.

importantKeywords.definition: A factor that is a prime number.

topics.quickSummary: Large numbers can be written in a short exponent form, like 10 to the power 5 = 1,00,000, and numbers can be written as products of primes using exponents, such as 72 = 2^3 x 3^2.

topics.topic: Multiplying Powers with the Same Base
topics.meaning: When multiplying powers of the same base, we add the exponents.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There are simple rules, called the laws of exponents, that make working with exponents easier. The first law deals with multiplication: when we multiply two powers that have the same base, we keep the base and add the exponents. In symbols, a to the m multiplied by a to the n equals a to the (m + n). For example, 2 to the power 3 multiplied by 2 to the power 2 equals 2 to the power (3 + 2) = 2 to the power 5 = 32. This law works because the total number of times the base is multiplied is just the sum of the two exponents. This rule saves us from expanding everything and is very useful.
topics.easyExplanation: To multiply powers with the same base, add the exponents: a^m x a^n = a^(m+n). So 2^3 x 2^2 = 2^5 = 32.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same base
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This law applies to powers with the same base.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add the exponents
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep the base and add the exponents: a^m x a^n = a^(m+n).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why it works
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The base is multiplied a total of (m + n) times.

examples: 2 to the power 3 x 2 to the power 2 = 2 to the power 5 = 32.
a^m x a^n = a^(m+n).

importantKeywords.definition: A rule for operating on powers.
The number being raised to a power.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Same base is needed for this law.

importantKeywords.definition: The power a base is raised to.

topics.quickSummary: When multiplying powers with the same base, we keep the base and add the exponents, so a^m x a^n = a^(m+n) and 2^3 x 2^2 = 2^5 = 32.

topics.topic: Dividing Powers with the Same Base
topics.meaning: When dividing powers of the same base, we subtract the exponents.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Another law of exponents deals with division: when we divide two powers that have the same base, we keep the base and subtract the exponent of the denominator from the exponent of the numerator. In symbols, a to the m divided by a to the n equals a to the (m - n). For example, 2 to the power 5 divided by 2 to the power 2 equals 2 to the power (5 - 2) = 2 to the power 3 = 8. This law works because dividing cancels some of the repeated factors. A special result follows: any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1, because a to the m divided by a to the m equals a to the power 0, which is 1.
topics.easyExplanation: To divide powers with the same base, subtract the exponents: a^m ÷ a^n = a^(m-n). So 2^5 ÷ 2^2 = 2^3 = 8. Any non-zero number to the power 0 is 1.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same base
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This law applies to powers with the same base.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Subtract the exponents
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep the base and subtract: a^m ÷ a^n = a^(m-n).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Power zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1.

examples: 2 to the power 5 ÷ 2 to the power 2 = 2 to the power 3 = 8.
Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1, like 7^0 = 1.

importantKeywords.definition: A rule for operating on powers.
Any non-zero number to the power 0 is 1.

topics.quickSummary: When dividing powers with the same base, we keep the base and subtract the exponents, so a^m ÷ a^n = a^(m-n) and 2^5 ÷ 2^2 = 2^3 = 8; any non-zero number to the power 0 is 1.

topics.topic: Power of a Power and Other Laws
topics.meaning: When taking a power of a power, we multiply the exponents, and powers of products and quotients follow similar laws.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: More laws of exponents help with other situations. When we take a power of a power, we keep the base and multiply the exponents; in symbols, (a to the m) to the n equals a to the (m x n). For example, (2 to the power 3) to the power 2 equals 2 to the power 6 = 64. There are also laws for products and quotients: the power of a product equals the product of the powers, so (a x b) to the m equals a to the m multiplied by b to the m; and the power of a quotient is handled in a similar way. These laws, together with the rules for multiplying and dividing powers, let us simplify any expression with exponents quickly.
topics.easyExplanation: Power of a power: multiply the exponents, (a^m)^n = a^(m x n), so (2^3)^2 = 2^6 = 64. Powers of products: (a x b)^m = a^m x b^m.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Power of a power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep the base and multiply the exponents: (a^m)^n = a^(m x n).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Power of a product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: (a x b)^m = a^m x b^m.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify quickly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These laws let us simplify exponent expressions quickly.

examples: (2 to the power 3) to the power 2 = 2 to the power 6 = 64.
(a x b)^m = a^m x b^m.

importantKeywords.definition: A rule for operating on powers.

topics.quickSummary: When taking a power of a power, we multiply the exponents, (a^m)^n = a^(m x n), and the power of a product equals the product of the powers, (a x b)^m = a^m x b^m.
revisionNotes: Power of a power: multiply exponents.
Power of a product: (a x b)^m = a^m x b^m.

topics.topic: Expressing Numbers in Expanded Form Using Exponents
topics.meaning: Numbers can be written in expanded form using powers of 10 to show the place value of each digit.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can write any number in expanded form using powers of 10, which clearly shows the place value of each digit. The place values are powers of 10: the ones place is 10 to the power 0 (which is 1), the tens place is 10 to the power 1, the hundreds place is 10 to the power 2, the thousands place is 10 to the power 3, and so on. For example, 4,572 can be written as 4 x 10^3 + 5 x 10^2 + 7 x 10^1 + 2 x 10^0. Writing numbers this way using exponents makes the structure of our number system clear and connects place value with powers of 10. It is also the first step towards writing numbers in standard form.
topics.easyExplanation: Numbers can be written in expanded form using powers of 10: 4,572 = 4 x 10^3 + 5 x 10^2 + 7 x 10^1 + 2 x 10^0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Expanded form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each digit is multiplied by its power of 10.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Shows structure
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This shows the structure of the number system clearly.

topics.meaning: Standard form writes very large numbers as a number between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Very large numbers are difficult to write and read in full, so we use the standard form (also called scientific notation). In standard form, a number is written as a number between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10. For example, 59,000 is written as 5.9 x 10 to the power 4, and the speed of light, about 300,000,000 m/s, is written as 3 x 10 to the power 8. To write a number in standard form, we place the decimal point after the first digit and count how many places the point has moved to find the power of 10. Standard form is widely used in science to handle very large numbers neatly and to compare them easily.
topics.easyExplanation: Standard form writes a big number as a value between 1 and 10 times a power of 10. So 59,000 = 5.9 x 10^4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why use it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Standard form makes very large numbers easy to write and read.

topics.meaning: Exponents are used to write and compare very large and very small quantities in science and daily life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Exponents and standard form are very useful in real life, especially in science, where we deal with extremely large and small numbers. For example, the distance of the Sun from the Earth, the number of stars in a galaxy, the size of tiny cells, and the population of the world are all easier to write and compare using exponents and standard form. Exponents also appear in measuring units (like square and cubic units), in computer memory sizes, and in many formulas. By turning long numbers into short exponent forms, we can write them quickly, compare them at a glance (by comparing the powers of 10), and use them in calculations. This shows how exponents make handling big and small numbers practical.

topics.meaning: A figure has line symmetry if a line divides it into two identical mirror-image halves.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has line symmetry (also called reflection symmetry) if there is a line that divides it into two parts that are mirror images of each other. This line is called the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry. If we fold the figure along this line, the two halves match exactly. Many letters, shapes and natural objects have line symmetry, such as the letter A, a butterfly and a leaf. A figure may have no line of symmetry, one line, or several lines. Finding lines of symmetry helps us understand balanced and beautiful shapes, which is why symmetry is important in art, design and nature.
topics.easyExplanation: A figure has line symmetry if a line splits it into two mirror-image halves that match when folded. The letter A and a butterfly have line symmetry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What line symmetry is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A line divides the figure into two mirror-image halves.

topics.topic: Figures with Multiple Lines of Symmetry
topics.meaning: Some figures have more than one line of symmetry.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure can have not just one but several lines of symmetry. For example, a rectangle has 2 lines of symmetry (one through the midpoints of each pair of opposite sides). A square has 4 lines of symmetry (two through the midpoints of opposite sides and two along the diagonals). An equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry. A circle has countless (infinite) lines of symmetry, because any line through its centre divides it into two matching halves. To find all the lines of symmetry of a figure, we look for every line along which the figure can be folded so the two halves match. The number of lines of symmetry depends on the shape.
topics.easyExplanation: Some figures have several lines of symmetry: a rectangle has 2, a square has 4, an equilateral triangle has 3, and a circle has countless.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find them all
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Look for every fold line that makes the two halves match.

topics.topic: Lines of Symmetry in Regular Polygons
topics.meaning: A regular polygon has as many lines of symmetry as it has sides.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A regular polygon is a closed figure with all sides equal and all angles equal, such as an equilateral triangle, a square, a regular pentagon and a regular hexagon. A special property of regular polygons is that the number of lines of symmetry is equal to the number of sides. So an equilateral triangle (3 sides) has 3 lines of symmetry, a square (4 sides) has 4 lines of symmetry, a regular pentagon (5 sides) has 5 lines of symmetry, and a regular hexagon (6 sides) has 6 lines of symmetry. As the number of sides increases, the number of lines of symmetry increases, and a circle (with 'infinitely many sides') has countless lines of symmetry. This neat rule helps us count the lines of symmetry of any regular polygon.
topics.easyExplanation: A regular polygon has as many lines of symmetry as its number of sides: a square (4 sides) has 4, a regular pentagon (5 sides) has 5.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A regular polygon has all sides and all angles equal.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The number of lines of symmetry equals the number of sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: More sides
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: More sides means more lines of symmetry; a circle has countless.

topics.meaning: Line symmetry is closely connected to reflection, where one half is the mirror image of the other.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Line symmetry is really about reflection. When a figure has a line of symmetry, one half of it is the reflection (mirror image) of the other half across that line. We can think of the line of symmetry as a mirror placed on the figure: the part on one side and its reflection on the other side together make the whole figure. This is why each point on one side has a matching point on the other side, at the same distance from the line. Understanding symmetry as reflection helps us complete symmetric figures: if we are given one half of a symmetric figure and the line of symmetry, we can draw the other half as the mirror image. Reflection and line symmetry go hand in hand.
topics.easyExplanation: Line symmetry is reflection: one half is the mirror image of the other across the line. We can complete a symmetric figure by drawing the mirror image.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Symmetry is reflection

topics.meaning: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after being turned by less than a full turn about its centre.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides line symmetry, figures can have rotational symmetry. A figure has rotational symmetry if, when it is rotated (turned) about a fixed central point by some angle less than a full turn, it looks exactly the same as it did at the start. The fixed central point about which the figure is turned is called the centre of rotation. For example, a square looks the same after being turned a quarter turn (90 degrees), so it has rotational symmetry. A windmill or a fan with equal blades also has rotational symmetry. Every figure looks the same after one complete turn, but only special figures look the same before completing the full turn; those have rotational symmetry.
topics.easyExplanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a partial turn about its centre. A square looks the same after a quarter turn.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rotate the figure about a fixed point, the centre of rotation.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If it looks the same after a turn less than full, it has rotational symmetry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Example
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A square looks the same after a quarter turn (90 degrees).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Special figures
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only special figures match before a full turn; those have rotational symmetry.

topics.meaning: The order of rotational symmetry is the number of times a figure looks the same during one full turn.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a figure has rotational symmetry, we describe it by its order of rotational symmetry, which is the number of times the figure looks exactly the same during one complete turn (360 degrees). For example, a square looks the same 4 times in a full turn (after each 90-degree turn), so it has rotational symmetry of order 4. An equilateral triangle has order 3, and a rectangle has order 2. The angle of rotation that brings the figure to look the same is 360 degrees divided by the order. A figure that only looks the same after a full turn has order 1, which means it has no real rotational symmetry. The order tells us how strong the rotational symmetry is.
topics.easyExplanation: The order of rotational symmetry is how many times a figure looks the same in one full turn. A square has order 4; an equilateral triangle has order 3.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The matching angle is 360 degrees divided by the order.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Order 1
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A figure with order 1 has no real rotational symmetry.

topics.topic: Figures with Both Kinds of Symmetry
topics.meaning: Some figures have both line symmetry and rotational symmetry.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure can have line symmetry, rotational symmetry, both, or neither. Many regular shapes have both kinds of symmetry. For example, a square has 4 lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order 4; an equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order 3; and a circle has countless lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of every order. On the other hand, some figures have only one kind, for example the letter S has rotational symmetry (order 2) but no line of symmetry, while the letter A has line symmetry but no rotational symmetry. Looking for both kinds of symmetry helps us describe a figure fully.
topics.easyExplanation: Some figures have both line and rotational symmetry: a square has 4 lines and order 4. The letter S has rotational symmetry but no line of symmetry.

topics.meaning: Symmetry is found everywhere in nature, art, architecture and design.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Symmetry is not just a mathematical idea; it is all around us. In nature, we see symmetry in the wings of a butterfly, the petals of a flower, the shape of leaves, snowflakes and the human body. In art and design, symmetry is used to make patterns, rangolis, jewellery and decorations look balanced and beautiful. In architecture, many buildings, such as the Taj Mahal, are built with symmetry to look grand and pleasing. Symmetry is also used in making objects work properly, like the balanced blades of a fan. Noticing symmetry around us helps us connect mathematics to the real world and appreciate the beauty in shapes and patterns.

topics.meaning: Plane figures are two-dimensional (flat), while solid shapes are three-dimensional and occupy space.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Shapes are of two kinds. Plane figures are two-dimensional (2D); they have only length and breadth and lie flat, like squares, rectangles, triangles and circles. Solid shapes are three-dimensional (3D); they have length, breadth and height (or depth) and take up space, like cubes, cuboids, cylinders, cones, spheres and pyramids. Many everyday objects are solid shapes: a dice is a cube, a ball is a sphere, a tin can is a cylinder, and an ice-cream cone is a cone. Being able to tell the difference between 2D and 3D shapes and to recognise common solids is the first step in studying solid shapes.
topics.easyExplanation: Plane figures are flat 2D shapes (square, circle); solid shapes are 3D and take up space (cube, sphere, cylinder). A dice is a cube; a ball is a sphere.

topics.meaning: Solid shapes are described by their faces (surfaces), edges (where faces meet) and vertices (corners).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Every solid shape can be described by counting three things: its faces, edges and vertices. A face is a flat (or curved) surface of the solid; an edge is the line segment where two faces meet; and a vertex is a corner point where edges meet. For example, a cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, and a cuboid has the same counts. A cylinder has 2 flat faces and 1 curved face, while a cone has 1 flat face, 1 curved face and 1 vertex. A sphere has just 1 curved face and no edges or vertices. Counting faces, edges and vertices helps us identify and compare different solid shapes.
topics.easyExplanation: Solids have faces (surfaces), edges (where faces meet) and vertices (corners). A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.

topics.meaning: Prisms and pyramids are special kinds of solid shapes with flat faces.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two important families of solid shapes are prisms and pyramids. A prism is a solid that has two identical and parallel flat ends (called bases) joined by rectangular faces; for example, a cuboid is a prism with rectangular bases, and a triangular prism has triangular bases. A pyramid is a solid that has one flat base and triangular faces that meet at a single point at the top, called the apex; for example, a square pyramid has a square base, and a triangular pyramid (tetrahedron) has a triangular base. Prisms and pyramids are named after the shape of their base. Recognising prisms and pyramids helps us classify and understand many solid shapes around us.

topics.meaning: A net is a flat shape that can be folded to make a solid shape.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A net is a flat (2D) shape that can be cut out and folded to form a three-dimensional (3D) solid. Each solid shape has a net made of its faces joined along edges. For example, the net of a cube is made of six squares joined together in a particular way so that they fold into a cube; a cuboid's net is made of six rectangles; a cylinder's net is made of two circles and a rectangle; and a cone's net is made of a circle and a curved shape (a sector). A single solid can sometimes have more than one possible net. Studying nets helps us understand how solid shapes are built from flat faces, and it is useful for making boxes, packets and models.

topics.topic: Drawing Solids on a Flat Surface
topics.meaning: Solid (3D) shapes can be drawn on flat (2D) paper using sketches like oblique and isometric drawings.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although solid shapes are three-dimensional, we often need to draw them on flat, two-dimensional paper. To make a flat drawing look like a solid, we use special ways of drawing. In an oblique sketch, we draw the front face of the solid in its true shape and then draw the depth using slanting parallel lines, which gives a quick 3D look. In an isometric sketch, drawn on special dotted (isometric) paper, all the edges are drawn to their correct lengths along three directions, giving a more accurate 3D drawing. These drawings let us show solids like cubes and cuboids on paper. Learning to draw solids helps us visualise and communicate 3D shapes clearly.
topics.easyExplanation: Solids are drawn on flat paper using oblique sketches (front face true, depth slanting) and isometric sketches (on dotted paper, edges to true length).

topics.meaning: A solid can be seen from different directions, giving its top, front and side views.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we look at a solid shape from different directions, we see different flat (2D) shapes; these are called the views of the solid. The three main views are the top view (looking straight down from above), the front view (looking straight from the front), and the side view (looking from the side). Each view shows a different flat outline of the same solid. For example, the top view of a cylinder standing upright is a circle, while its front view is a rectangle. Engineers and designers use these views to describe and make objects accurately. Looking at the different views helps us understand the true shape of a solid and how it would look from each direction.
topics.easyExplanation: A solid seen from different directions gives its top, front and side views, each a flat shape. A standing cylinder's top view is a circle; its front view is a rectangle.

topics.meaning: A cross-section is the flat shape we get when we cut straight through a solid.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A cross-section is the new flat (2D) shape that appears when we cut straight through a solid shape. The shape of the cross-section depends on the solid and on how we cut it. For example, if we cut a cylinder straight across (horizontally), the cross-section is a circle, but if we cut it down the middle (vertically), the cross-section is a rectangle. If we cut a cube straight across, the cross-section is a square. Looking at cross-sections is a good way to understand the inside of a solid and how it is built. Cross-sections are used in science, geography (like cross-sections of the Earth) and in many real applications.
topics.easyExplanation: A cross-section is the flat shape we get by cutting straight through a solid. Cutting a cylinder across gives a circle; cutting a cube across gives a square.

topics.meaning: When light shines on a solid, it casts a shadow that is a flat (2D) shape.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When light falls on a solid (3D) object, the object blocks the light and casts a shadow, which is always a flat, two-dimensional shape. The shape of the shadow depends on the solid and on the direction of the light. For example, a ball (sphere) always casts a circular shadow, while a cylinder can cast a circular shadow or a rectangular shadow depending on how the light falls on it, and a cube can cast a square shadow. So a single solid can cast different shadows from different directions, just as it has different views. Studying shadows is another way to connect 3D solids with 2D shapes and to understand the shapes of solids. It is also a fun way to explore solids using a torch and a screen.
topics.easyExplanation: A solid casts a flat shadow when light shines on it. A ball casts a circular shadow; a cube can cast a square shadow, depending on the light's direction.
